Keywords: solution, feature, replace, build, implement, add
Symptom: The problem statement describes a feature or system to build instead of a user pain.
Root Cause: PM jumps to solution before validating the problem. Org pressure to deliver a specific feature.
Impact: Kills creative alternatives. Team builds what was asked even if it solves nothing. Wastes entire engineering cycle if the assumption is wrong.
Bad Example: Problem: In Q3 we need to replace the old email notification system with a new one.
Good Example: Problem: 10% of notification emails fail to reach passengers daily. This causes 24% of affected passengers to call support, loading it to 85% capacity, and results in 2% trip cancellations plus 16 litigation cases per quarter.
Fix: Rewrite by asking: 'What pain does the user experience today?' Add quantified data. Remove any mention of a specific solution.

Keywords: vague, unclear, many, some, lot, often, sometimes, most
Symptom: Numbers are absent or described vaguely: 'many users', 'often fails', 'causes frustration'.
Root Cause: PM relies on intuition rather than data. Research was skipped.
Impact: No baseline means you can't measure success. Stakeholders can't assess ROI. Engineering can't prioritize.
Bad Example: Problem: Many users have trouble completing onboarding.
Good Example: Problem: 62% of users who sign up abandon onboarding before completing step 3. Exit surveys show confusion at the 'connect your data source' step. This costs us ~140 potential activations per week at current signup volume.
Fix: Add specific numbers: percentages, counts, dollar amounts, time impacts. Source: user research, analytics, support tickets.

Keywords: no ac, no criteria, acceptance, done, testable, verify, complete
Symptom: User stories exist but have no acceptance criteria or only vague ones like 'works correctly'.
Root Cause: Time pressure. PM assumes dev and QA will figure out what 'done' means.
Impact: Endless 'is it done?' debates. Rework after sprint review. QA doesn't know what to test. Different definitions of done across team members.
Bad Example: US-1: As a user, I want to import CSV files. [No AC]
Good Example: US-1 AC: System accepts CSV files up to 50MB. Given invalid encoding, error shown: 'File encoding not supported. Please save as UTF-8.' Import of 10,000 rows completes in <5 minutes. Duplicate rows trigger a warning with count and skip/overwrite choice.
Fix: For every user story, add at least 3 acceptance criteria. Use Given/When/Then for complex flows. Use checklist for binary conditions.

Keywords: priority, p0, must have, critical, all important, everything urgent
Symptom: Every requirement is marked P0 or Must Have — no meaningful prioritization.
Root Cause: PM is conflict-averse about cutting scope. Stakeholders added items without pushback. Scope wasn't controlled.
Impact: Engineers can't make tradeoff decisions autonomously. Every descoped item feels like a failure. Launch dates slip because nothing can be deferred.
Bad Example: Requirements: FR-1 P0, FR-2 P0, FR-3 P0, FR-4 P0, FR-5 P0...
Good Example: Requirements: FR-1 P0 (launch blocker): Accept CSV uploads. FR-2 P0 (launch blocker): Basic duplicate detection. FR-3 P1 (ship within 2 weeks): Advanced field mapping. FR-4 P2 (future): Scheduled imports.
Fix: Force yourself to have no more than 3-5 P0 requirements per story. If everything is P0, start over. Frame as: 'What must be true on day 1 for this to be usable?'

Keywords: edge case, error, failure, exception, boundary, negative, what if
Symptom: PRD documents only the success path. Error states, boundary conditions, and failure modes are absent.
Root Cause: PM focuses on the ideal user journey. Edge cases feel like 'engineering's problem'.
Impact: Most production bugs come from unspecified edge cases. Support tickets spike. On-call incidents from unhandled failure modes. Costly post-launch patches.
Bad Example: Feature: User can upload a profile photo. [No edge cases documented]
Good Example: Feature: User can upload a profile photo. Edge cases: File >5MB: Show error. Non-image file: Show 'Only JPG, PNG, and GIF files are accepted.' Network failure mid-upload: Show retry message. User navigates away: Confirm dialog before leaving.
Fix: For each feature, run a 10-minute edge case brainstorm: What if the input is wrong? What if the network fails? What if the user has no data? What if they're on a slow connection?

Keywords: scope, exclusion, not included, future, phase 2, won't
Symptom: The PRD has no explicit list of what is NOT included in this release.
Root Cause: PM assumes everyone knows what's excluded. Scope creep is not anticipated.
Impact: Scope creep mid-development. Stakeholders add 'obvious' features late in the sprint. Engineering builds more than intended. Launch delays.
Bad Example: [Section does not exist in the PRD]
Good Example: ## Out of Scope (V1): Scheduled/recurring imports (Phase 2), Google Sheets sync (separate initiative), Excel format support (not planned), Mobile app import (post-GA).
Fix: Write the out-of-scope section before you write the requirements. Ask in every stakeholder review: 'Is there anything missing?' Then document deferred items explicitly.
Source: River PRD Guide / Best PM Jobs

Keywords: collaboration, engineering, design, stakeholder, review, input, feedback
Symptom: PM writes the entire PRD alone and hands it to engineering as a finished artifact.
Root Cause: PM treats PRD as their document, not a team artifact. No engineering review before finalization.
Impact: Technical feasibility issues discovered mid-sprint. Engineering proposes better solutions that weren't considered. PRD misses important constraints. Trust between PM and engineering erodes.
Bad Example: PM writes full spec -> sends to Eng -> Eng finds major technical blocker in sprint -> rework.
Good Example: PM drafts problem statement + user stories -> shares with tech lead for 30-min review -> tech lead flags that proposed solution conflicts with existing API architecture -> PRD is updated before sprint starts.
Fix: Share PRD with engineering lead at the 70% draft stage, before requirements are final. Ask: 'Are there simpler ways to achieve this outcome?' and 'What technical constraints should I know about?'

Keywords: goal, requirement, objective, feature, mix, confuse, combine
Symptom: Goals and requirements are written in the same section or in the same language.
Root Cause: PM doesn't distinguish between the strategic why (goal) and the functional what (requirement).
Impact: Teams lose sight of the purpose. Requirements get added without testing against the goal. Metrics discussions are derailed because outcomes and features are conflated.
Bad Example: Goal: Add a bulk import feature so users can upload CSV files with contact data.
Good Example: Goal: Increase enterprise trial-to-paid conversion by 25% by removing the manual data entry barrier during onboarding. Requirement FR-1: The system must allow users to upload a CSV file with contact data (up to 50MB, up to 10,000 rows).
Fix: Goals describe WHY (outcomes). Requirements describe WHAT (behaviors). Keep them in separate sections. Test each requirement against the goal.

Keywords: epic, too large, too big, sprint, velocity, story size, multiple features
Symptom: A single user story covers multiple features, user types, or workflows.
Root Cause: PM bundles related features to avoid writing multiple stories. Effort not estimated before writing.
Impact: Story can't be completed in a sprint. Partial implementations create awkward UI states. No clear definition of done for the full story. Estimation is impossible.
Bad Example: As a user, I want to sign up, complete onboarding, and set up my team so that I can start using the product.
Good Example: Story 1: As a new user, I want to sign up with my email. Story 2: As a signed-up user, I want to complete a 3-step product tour. Story 3: As an account owner, I want to invite my teammates.
Fix: If a story contains 'and', 'also', 'then', or can't be finished in one sprint, split it. Apply INVEST: can the story be completed independently and estimated in isolation?

Keywords: generic, user, as a user, vague, persona, who, audience, target
Symptom: Stories use 'As a user' instead of a specific role or persona.
Root Cause: PM hasn't done persona research. Shortcut taken to save time. Assumes all users are the same.
Impact: Engineers can't empathize with the use case. Edge cases specific to a role are missed. Design optimizes for nobody in particular. Lowest-common-denominator features result.
Bad Example: As a user, I want to see a dashboard.
Good Example: As a sales manager overseeing a team of 8 reps, I want to see my team's weekly activity metrics at a glance so that I can identify coaching opportunities without running separate reports.
Fix: Replace 'As a user' with a specific role from your persona list. Add one sentence of context about what makes this persona's situation distinct.

Keywords: so that, why, value, benefit, purpose, reason, outcome
Symptom: User stories end after 'I want [action]' with no 'so that [outcome]'.
Root Cause: PM treats the format as optional boilerplate. Goal: just get tickets written fast.
Impact: Team builds the feature with no understanding of its purpose. Wrong implementation choices are made. Features get deprioritized at planning because stakeholders can't assess value.
Bad Example: As a PM, I want to export PRD data.
Good Example: As a PM preparing for a stakeholder review, I want to export my PRD as a PDF so that I can share a read-only formatted version with executives who don't have tool access.
Fix: The 'so that' clause is the business justification. Never skip it. If you can't write it, the story may not have clear user value.

Keywords: how, implementation, technical, use library, button color, pixel, code, architecture
Symptom: PRD specifies how to build the feature (code libraries, UI elements, database choices) instead of what it should do.
Root Cause: PM has technical background and over-reaches. Stakeholder pressure to specify a specific technology choice.
Impact: Constrains engineering unnecessarily. Often results in a suboptimal solution. Engineers feel their expertise is not trusted. Creates brittle requirements that break when technology changes.
Bad Example: The import feature must use a Redis queue with Bull.js, process in batches of 1,000 rows, and use a PostgreSQL bulk insert.
Good Example: The import feature must process 10,000 rows in <5 minutes with progress updates every 10 seconds. Users must receive an email notification on completion or failure. The system must handle duplicate rows gracefully.
Fix: Specify WHAT (outcomes and constraints) not HOW (implementation). Replace technology names with behavior: 'processes asynchronously' not 'uses a queue'. 'Encrypts at rest' not 'uses AES-256'.

Keywords: guardrail, regression, protect, floor, threshold, don't break, side effect, unintended
Symptom: Success metrics exist but no guardrails are defined — nothing protecting against unintended regressions.
Root Cause: PM focused only on what to improve. Didn't think about what the change could break.
Impact: Metric-driven optimization causes regressions in adjacent metrics. Improving conversion by reducing friction might increase churn if the wrong users convert. No alarm is raised.
Good Example: Success: Increase free-to-paid conversion rate by 30%. Guardrails: D30 retention of converted users must not drop below current 65% baseline. Support ticket volume must not increase by more than 10%.
Fix: Before launching, ask: 'What are the 3 metrics this feature could accidentally make worse?' Add them as guardrails with explicit thresholds.

Keywords: stale, outdated, old, version, changelog, change, update, drift
Symptom: The PRD is not updated as requirements change during development. The current state of the feature diverges from the document.
Root Cause: PM treats PRD as a one-time artifact. No process for updating it. Too busy during development sprint.
Impact: New team members read outdated requirements. Post-mortem confusion about what was actually decided. Future PRDs built on wrong assumptions about the current state.
Bad Example: PRD last updated: 3 months ago (development started 2 months ago).
Good Example: PRD includes a Changelog section. Every change is logged: Date, Author, What changed, Why. Requirements updated within 24h of any agreed scope change.
Fix: Add a Changelog section to every PRD template. Make updating the PRD part of the definition of done for any scope change.

Keywords: metrics, kpi, measure, success, goal, outcome
Symptom: PRD has a goals section but no specific, measurable targets with baselines and timeframes.
Root Cause: PM isn't sure what data is available. Metrics are seen as engineering's problem. Launch pressure skips measurement planning.
Impact: Team can't evaluate if the feature succeeded. Post-launch discussion is subjective. Feature can't be iterated on because there's no baseline. Can't justify future investment.
Bad Example: Goal: Improve the onboarding experience for new users.
Good Example: Primary metric: Increase onboarding completion rate from 40% to 70% within 60 days of launch. Measurement: Track via 'onboarding_completed' event in Mixpanel. Report weekly. Leading indicator: % of users reaching step 2 within first session (currently 55%).
Fix: For each goal, add: 'How will we measure this? What's the current baseline? What's the target? By when?' If you don't have a baseline, instrument measurement before the feature ships.

Keywords: long, verbose, bloated, wall of text, pages, encyclopedia, nobody reads
Symptom: PRD is 20+ pages with extensive prose, multiple appendices, and detailed explanations of things engineering already knows.
Root Cause: PM is thorough but not deliberate about audience. Treats length as a proxy for quality.
Impact: Nobody reads the full document. Key decisions are buried in prose. Engineering skips to the end. Inconsistency between what's in the PRD and what gets built.
Bad Example: 20-page PRD with full competitive analysis, user research transcripts, and complete database schema inline.
Good Example: 2-4 page core PRD with links to supporting documents: user research in Notion, competitive analysis in a separate doc, engineering design in a tech spec.
Fix: Aim for 2-4 pages for a standard feature PRD. Use progressive disclosure: core requirements in the PRD, detailed research and tech specs linked externally.
Source: River PRD Guide / Best PM Jobs

Keywords: dependency, depends, blocked, blocker, team, service, upstream, downstream, prerequisite
Symptom: PRD is written without checking what other teams, services, or features it depends on — or what depends on it.
Root Cause: PM writes in a silo. Dependencies are discovered mid-sprint.
Impact: Launch date slips because a dependency team wasn't looped in. Feature is built but can't ship because of an incomplete upstream dependency. Surprise integrations require re-architecture.
Good Example: Dependencies: Assessment Engine upgrade (owner: @platform-team, status: In QA, risk: HIGH). New ML microservice (owner: @ml-team, status: Not started, risk: MEDIUM — can prototype with rule-based logic as fallback).
Fix: Before finalizing any PRD, run a dependency discovery meeting with engineering. Ask: 'What does this feature need to exist, and what features will break if this changes?'
